name: Ciudad Real and Alarcos Loop
name_original: Alcolea de Calatrava desde Ciudad Real – circular por Parque arqueológico de Alarcos
description: Moderate 39.0-mile road cycling route from Ciudad Real, exploring the historical Alarcos Archaeological Park and volcanic landscapes.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Campo De Calatrava traverse a distinctive volcanic landscape, characterized by undulating roads and varied topography. The region features extensive agricultural fields, vineyards, and olive groves, providing tranquil passages through the rural Spanish countryside. Cyclists can expect a blend of flatter sections and climbs, with routes often passing through historic towns and offering views of ancient castles. This terrain makes Campo De Calatrava appealing for road cyclists seeking diverse challenges and scenic backdrops. Best road cycling routes around Campo De Calatrava The most popular road cycling route is Carrión de Calatrava from Ciudad Real – circular via Bolaños and Almagro, a 48.2 miles (77.6 km) moderate trail that takes 3 hours 8 minutes to complete. This circular route connects several historic towns, offering cultural stops along the way. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Ciudad Real and Alarcos Loop, a moderate 39.0 miles (62.8 km) path. This loop provides a balanced ride around Ciudad Real, featuring varied terrain and historical points of interest. Local road cyclists also love the Río Jabalón por Poblete y Baños de Calatrava — circular desde Ciudad Real, a 29.3 miles (47.1 km) trail leading through the scenic Río Jabalón area, often completed in about 1 hour 57 minutes. Road cycling around Campo De Calatrava is defined by its unique volcanic terrain, agricultural vistas, and historic towns. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides along river paths to more challenging routes with significant elevation gain. The routes in Campo De Calatrava are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from more than 40 reviews. More than 1500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Campo De Calatrava's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Campo de Calatrava?
A: Campo de Calatrava offers a unique road cycling experience with its distinctive volcanic landscape. You'll find undulating paved roads, interspersed with flatter sections and climbs, providing panoramic views. Routes often wind through extensive agricultural fields, vineyards, and olive groves, offering tranquil passages through the rural Spanish countryside.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Campo de Calatrava?
A: There are over 90 road cycling routes in Campo de Calatrava. These routes cater to various skill levels, including 21 easy, 56 moderate, and 13 difficult options, ensuring there's a suitable ride for almost every cyclist.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Campo de Calatrava?
A: The best seasons for road cycling in Campo de Calatrava are spring and autumn. During these times, temperatures are more moderate, allowing cyclists to avoid the intense heat of summer and the potentially cold winters typical of the Castilla-La Mancha region.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Campo de Calatrava offers 21 easy road cycling routes. A good option for a more relaxed ride is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1251843322/rio-jabalon-por-poblete-y-banos-de-calatrava-circular-desde-ciudad-real" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Jabalón por Poblete y Baños de Calatrava — circular desde Ciudad Real&lt;/a&gt;, which is 29.3 miles (47.1 km) long and leads through the scenic Río Jabalón area.

Q: What historical sites can I see along the road cycling routes in Campo de Calatrava?
A: Road cycling routes in Campo de Calatrava frequently pass through historic towns and lead to ancient castles, offering cultural stops. You can explore sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3882572" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alarcos Archaeological Park&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2661834" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calatrava la Vieja Archaeological Site&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5631925" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Caracuel&lt;/a&gt;. The region's name is linked to the medieval Order of Calatrava, with many villages and castles reflecting this rich history.

Q: Are there any natural features or scenic viewpoints to enjoy while cycling?
A: Yes, the volcanic landscape provides numerous opportunities for panoramic views. You can also encounter natural features like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3710191" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna del Prado&lt;/a&gt;, a saline volcanic lagoon, or enjoy views of the Guadiana River, particularly near the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1654225" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iron Bridge over the Guadiana River&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes in the region?
A: Yes, many routes in Campo de Calatrava are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1084271225/carrion-de-calatrava-from-ciudad-real-circular-via-bolanos-and-almagro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carrión de Calatrava from Ciudad Real – circular via Bolaños and Almagro&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1083958980/ciudad-real-and-alarcos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ciudad Real and Alarcos Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Campo de Calatrava?
A: The road cycling routes in Campo de Calatrava are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 1500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the region's varied terrain, often praising the unique volcanic scenery and the blend of cultural and natural attractions.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking a challenge, there are 13 difficult routes. One such route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1251860322/campo-de-calatrava-and-valle-de-alcudia-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 84.7 miles (136.3 km) with significant elevation gain, offering a demanding yet rewarding experience.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay near the cycling routes?
A: While specific cafes, pubs, or accommodations are not detailed for each route, many routes pass through or near historic towns such as Ciudad Real, Almagro, and Bolaños de Calatrava. These towns typically offer various services, including dining options and places to stay, making them convenient stops during or after your ride.

Q: Is parking available at the start of the routes?
A: Most road cycling routes in Campo de Calatrava start from towns or cities like Ciudad Real or Almagro. In these locations, you can typically find public parking facilities, though availability may vary. It's advisable to check local parking options for your chosen starting point.

Q: What is the longest road cycling route in Campo de Calatrava?
A: The longest route listed is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1251860322/campo-de-calatrava-and-valle-de-alcudia-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campo de Calatrava and Valle de Alcudia Loop&lt;/a&gt;, spanning 84.7 miles (136.3 km) with over 1100 meters of elevation gain, making it a significant challenge for endurance cyclists.
tips: It is the only door that is preserved today, of the seven that the old wall that surrounded Ciudad Real had. It had various functions throughout its history, serving from the patrol gate for the surveillance of the city, to portazgo or gate where taxes were paid in the transport of goods and people from Toledo to Seville.

Las Casas is an annex to the Spanish municipality of Ciudad Real, in the province of Ciudad Real. The town is located northwest of Ciudad Real, on the CM-403 road, towards Porzuna. It limits to the northwest with Picón and in its vicinity passes the Guadiana river and is the El Vicario Reservoir.

It was developed during the second third of the 16th century within the style called "Renaissance Purism"
Located in Pl. de los Reyes -Alcolea de Calatrava, Cdad. Real

The old bridge of Alarcos is located in a natural confinement of the Guadiana river. Throughout history, it has meant an important step since, since Roman times, it has been integrated into the road that linked Toledo with Seville.
One of the functions of the Castle of Alarcos would be to protect the passage through this point.

It is part of the Alarcos-Calatrava Archaeological Park.
The castle stands at the top, built on an artificial platform. Its plan is rectangular and it has nine towers, seven square and two pentagonal in the prow. It was occupied by Iberians, Romans and Arabs.
In 1195 a great battle took place here, the Battle of Alarcos against the Almohads, in which Alfonso VIII had to flee wounded in one leg. After the war, Alarcos and its entourage remained in Almohad power, which delayed the Reconquest for several decades.
This Battle took place in the plain that today extends between Alarcos and Poblete.

The Alarcos hill is located on the left bank of the Guadiana river, 8 kilometers from Ciudad Real capital. Excavated since 1984, we find mainly archaeological remains that show the human occupation of this space from the Bronze Age to the High Middle Ages, with a long intermediate period of depopulation in Roman and Visigothic times.

A religious building dating from the 17th–20th centuries and mixes a popular style with a Latin cross plan. Its interior does not stand out for its extravagance but for its simplicity, with its mud walls and reinforced masonry in the corners. It has limestone ashlars and its central vault stands out, which is the most recent construction inside the Church.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Alcolea de Calatrava circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as moderate. While it features mostly paved surfaces suitable for racebikes, its distance of nearly 63 km and elevation gain of over 400 meters mean it requires good physical condition. It's not typically recommended for absolute beginners.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: You can expect mostly paved surfaces, making it ideal for road cycling. The route offers a blend of historical landscapes, such as the Alarcos Archaeological Park, and natural beauty, including volcanic features near Alcolea de Calatrava and views of the Guadiana River.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is rich in historical and natural sights. Key highlights include the Alarcos Archaeological Park with its Iberian oppidum and medieval castle, the Alarcos Bridge on the Guadiana River, and the Puerta de Toledo. You'll also pass by religious buildings like Our Lady of the Assumption (Alcolea) and the Church of Saint Mary Magdalene (Poblete).

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region can experience hot summers, so spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant seasons for cycling. During these times, temperatures are milder, making the longer distance and moderate climbs more enjoyable.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Since the route starts from Ciudad Real and is circular, you can typically find parking within Ciudad Real itself. For access closer to the Alarcos Archaeological Park, there are usually designated parking areas for visitors to the park.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for the Alarcos Archaeological Park?
faqs.answer: While cycling through the general area of the Alarcos Archaeological Park is usually open, specific exhibitions or facilities within the park might have entrance fees. It's advisable to check the official website of the park for the most current information on access and fees before your visit.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily for road cycling on public roads and paths. While dogs are generally allowed in public outdoor spaces, it's important to keep them on a leash, especially near historical sites or in areas with traffic. Always ensure your dog is comfortable with the distance and terrain if you plan to bring them along.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several official trails. You will find parts of the Camino Natural del Guadiana, Ruta de Alarcos, and Cañada Real Segoviana, among others, integrated into the cycling path.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or water stops along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through settlements like Las Casas and Alcolea de Calatrava, where you can typically find cafes, restaurants, and opportunities to refill water bottles. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the longer stretches between towns.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this road cycling trip?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and length, bring plenty of water, energy snacks, and basic bike repair tools. Wear appropriate cycling attire, including a helmet, and consider sun protection like sunscreen and sunglasses, especially during warmer months. A fully charged phone and a navigation device are also recommended.

faqs.question: Is this a circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is a circular loop, starting and ending in Ciudad Real, and passing through Alcolea de Calatrava and the Alarcos Archaeological Park.

waypoints.name: Alarcos Bridge on the Guadiana River
